title: Automating Coral Bleaching Detection with Deep Learning
summary: Developed and published research utilizing advanced deep learning architectures (CoralNet, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2) to automate the detection and quantification of coral bleaching, achieving 89% accuracy in marine biodiversity monitoring tasks.
abstract: Automated monitoring of coral reef ecosystems is critical for tracking the global impacts of rising ocean temperatures. This paper presents a deep learning-based framework to identify and quantify coral bleaching events from underwater imagery. Integrating model features from CoralNet, InceptionV3, and MobileNetV2 with localized patch classifier structures, we establish a robust computational pipeline that reduces manual annotation loads while maintaining high diagnostic precision.
methodology: Pretrained Backbones: Comparative validation of features extracted from CoralNet-v2, InceptionV3, and MobileNetV2 models.
Data Pipeline: Dense color correction and histogram matching to resolve lighting variances in underwater optical channels.
Classifier Calibration: Softmax classification heads trained on public and proprietary labeled benthic surveys with automated augmentation layers.
keyFindings: The ensemble model achieved an 89% overall classification accuracy for bleached vs. healthy coral structures. Deployed on high-performance computing resources, the pipeline processes extensive video transects in real-time, providing critical, automated insights for marine biodiversity conservation efforts.
